hero.title: The Evans of Clerkenwell and Islington
hero.subtitle: German, Irish, and London roots — costermongers, brass finishers, and the Blitz

media.alt: St John's Gate, Clerkenwell, photographed by Henry Dixon in 1880 — the medieval gateway to the Priory of St John of Jerusalem
media.caption: St John's Gate, Clerkenwell, 1880. Photograph by Henry Dixon. The gate stood at the heart of the neighbourhood where William Evans was born and worked as a brass finisher

media.alt: 1871 Census page showing the Knecht household at Pembroke Street, Islington — George Knecht from Germany, wife Eliza from Ireland, and six children including Dorothea age 10
media.caption: 1871 Census — the Knecht household on Pembroke Street, Islington. George Knecht, gas worker from Germany; wife Eliza Sarah Glasscock, born Ireland. Daughter Dorothea, age 10, was already working as an artificial flower maker

media.alt: 1901 Census showing the Evans household at 44 Pembroke Street, Islington — William Evans, wife Dorothea, and nine children including Alfred age 9
media.caption: 1901 Census — William Evans (43, brass trimmer) and Dorothea (40) with nine children at 44 Pembroke Street. The same street where Dorothea had grown up in the Knecht household thirty years earlier

media.alt: 1911 Census showing the Evans household at 124 Barnsbury Road, Islington — William Evans age 54, wife Dorothea age 50, married 32 years
media.caption: 1911 Census — William Evans (54, brass finisher) and Dorothea (50), married 32 years, signed by William himself. Alfred (19) is a jewellers assistant; eight children at home

media.alt: A London costermonger with his barrow, photographed by John Thomson for Street Life in London, 1877
media.caption: A London costermonger, 1877. Photograph by John Thomson. The 1921 Census recorded Alfred Evans as a Costermonger at 28 — already on the barrows, self-employed, at 4 Bratton Street, Islington

media.alt: Marriage register pages 6–7 from St David's Church, Islington — Alfred Evans and Annie Fensom, Christmas Day 1912
media.caption: St David's Church, Islington — marriage register, 25 December 1912. Alfred Evans (19, barman) married Annie Fensom (19). Father: William Evans, horse dealer

media.alt: Bomb damage in London during the Blitz, 1940 — Holborn suffered the highest rate of damage per capita in the country
media.caption: London Blitz, 1940. Joseph Evans was born on 28 August 1940 — ten days before the bombing began. Holborn suffered the highest bomb damage per capita in the entire country

media.alt: Walker House, Ossulston Estate, Somers Town — the Grade II listed council estate where the next generation of the Evans family settled
media.caption: Walker House, Ossulston Estate, Somers Town. Built 1929–37 by the LCC to replace slums. Helia Cerpa-Pérez settled here after exile from Chile

media.alt: 1939 National Register showing Dorothea Evans, widowed, living with sons Ernest and Francis in Islington
media.caption: 1939 Register — Dorothea Evans, widowed at approximately 78, living with sons Ernest (electrician) and Francis (bus conductor). William had died between 1921 and 1939
